title: The Technical Interview
scene: You are applying for an IT position. Brian, the team leader, is asking about your routine.

title: Daily Standup Meeting
scene: You are speaking in a daily standup and explaining what you worked on yesterday.

title: Production Incident
scene: A production issue happened and you need to explain what is happening and what you are doing next.

title: Email to Your Manager
scene: You need to write and explain a formal email to your manager about a task you completed yesterday.

title: System Setup Instructions
scene: A new team member joined and you are explaining the steps needed to set up the local development environment.

title: Code Review Discussion
scene: You are in a code review session and need to explain the changes you are currently making to a component.

title: Sprint Planning Meeting
scene: The team is planning the next sprint and you are explaining what tasks you worked on and what went wrong last sprint.

title: System Architecture Proposal
scene: You are presenting a new system architecture to the engineering team and explaining how each component works.

title: Client Escalation Call
scene: A client is upset about a service outage and you need to explain what is currently happening and what steps are being taken.

title: On-Call Shift Handover
scene: You are handing over the on-call rotation to a colleague and describing what was happening when your shift ended.

title: Introducing Yourself to the Team
scene: It is your first day at a new company. You are joining a remote team call and introducing yourself and your skills.

title: Writing a Status Update
scene: Your manager asks for a short written update on a task. You need to describe what you have done and what is still left.

title: Planning the Next Sprint
scene: The sprint planning session is starting and you are sharing what the team intends to complete in the coming two weeks.

title: Meeting Security Requirements
scene: You are explaining to a new developer the mandatory security practices that must be followed when handling user data.

title: Reviewing Completed Tasks
scene: The sprint just ended. You are reviewing with your manager what the team has accomplished and delivered.

title: Explaining Legacy Systems
scene: A new developer asks why certain parts of the codebase look outdated. You explain how the system used to work before the migration.

title: Code Quality Standards Discussion
scene: Your team is discussing how to improve code quality. You are recommending standards that every developer should and ought to follow.

title: Discussing What-If Scenarios
scene: During a team planning session you explore hypothetical scenarios — what would you do if a service failed, if load doubled, or if a key engineer left.

title: Comparing Frameworks
scene: Your team is choosing between two frontend frameworks. You are comparing their performance, scalability, and learning curve.

title: Documenting an API
scene: You have built a new REST API. You are describing how each endpoint is used and how data is exposed to external consumers.

title: Planning a Feature Rollout
scene: You are presenting a plan for rolling out a new feature. You explain what will happen at each stage if testing is successful.

title: Writing a Technical Proposal
scene: You are writing a proposal for a new caching strategy. You need to explain the goals, the approach, and what needs to be implemented.

title: Describing a System Outage
scene: During a post-incident review you are describing exactly what was happening in the system when the outage began.

title: Tracking Ongoing Refactoring
scene: You are giving a weekly status update on a long-running refactoring project. You explain what has been happening and for how long.

title: Committing to a Deadline
scene: Your manager asks for a firm commitment on a delivery date. You explain what the team will finish and by when.

title: Confirming Technical Requirements
scene: Before starting development you are confirming the technical requirements with the product owner to make sure nothing is missed.

title: Presenting a Microservices Design
scene: You are presenting a new microservices architecture. You explain which services handle which responsibilities and how they communicate.

title: Writing an Incident Post-Mortem
scene: You are presenting the findings of a formal post-mortem. You describe what had already happened by the time the incident was detected.

title: Analysing a Failed Deployment
scene: A production deployment failed and caused a two-hour outage. You are explaining what would have happened differently if the team had followed the runbook.

title: Summarising a Meeting
scene: You are writing a summary of a stakeholder meeting. You need to report what different participants said without quoting them directly.

title: Formal Presentation to the Board
scene: You are presenting your engineering team's quarterly results to the company board. You need to use formal and emphatic language.

title: Explaining Root Cause Analysis
scene: You are walking the team through the root cause analysis of a major incident, emphasising which component was responsible.

title: Reflecting on a Project That Failed
scene: A six-month project was cancelled due to technical and planning failures. You are reflecting on what you wish had been done differently.

title: Planning a Data Migration
scene: You are presenting the plan for a major database migration. You explain what data will be transferred, archived, or deleted during the cutover.

title: Post-Incident Review
scene: Following a series of incidents, you are leading a review that mixes past actions with their present consequences.

title: Quarterly Release Planning
scene: You are presenting the roadmap for Q3. You explain exactly what will have been completed by each milestone date.

title: Legal Compliance Meeting
scene: You are attending a GDPR compliance review with the legal team. You explain what is essential and what must be done in formal subjunctive language.

title: Documenting a User Research Session
scene: You conducted user interviews and are now writing up what participants were asked and how they responded.

title: Architecture Review with External Auditor
scene: An external auditor is reviewing your system design. You explain each component in detail, adding non-essential extra information about context.

title: Client Escalation Resolution
scene: A client escalation has been resolved but you need to debrief the account team, emphasising how serious and complex the situation was.

title: Risk Assessment Discussion
scene: Your team is assessing risks for an upcoming release. You discuss what might go wrong and which outcomes are possible under different scenarios.

title: Making Business Projections
scene: You are presenting projected engineering capacity and business impact to a non-technical audience.

title: User Research Interview
scene: You are conducting a structured research interview. You need to ask questions politely and indirectly to avoid leading the participant.

title: Managing Team Resources
scene: Your manager asks about headcount and capacity. You describe how many developers are available and whether the team has enough people.

title: Evaluating a Tech Solution
scene: You are reviewing a proposed technical solution with your team and assessing whether it is sufficient or whether it is excessive for the requirement.

title: Handling Server Issues
scene: The DevOps team is dealing with failing servers and you need to describe what actions are being taken using separable phrasal verbs.

title: Team Communication Breakdown
scene: You are describing a situation where miscommunication led to a bug. You explain how different people ran into issues or dealt with the problem.

title: Explaining How a System Works
scene: You are onboarding a new engineer and explaining the automatic behaviour of the system using factual, always-true statements.

title: Security Breach Post-Analysis
scene: You are presenting a post-mortem on a security breach and explaining that the attacker had been active for an extended period before detection.

title: Long-Term Project Status Report
scene: You are presenting a status report for a multi-year infrastructure project, highlighting how long certain work will have been in progress by the final deadline.
